source.title: Bootstrapper DevExp Pocket Manifest (draft)
source.content:
Purpose:
A compact, grab-and-go pocket manifesto for the SDK bootstrapper experience. This is intentionally opinionated: 10-minute demo, 30-minute prototype, and first-day stability checks that a new engineer or integrator can follow without deep prior knowledge of Redwood. The goal is to reduce cognitive load, surface obvious failure modes, and ship a reproducible first-success loop.

Audience / Personas:
- New engineer (backend/frontend) trying Redwood SDK for the first time.
- Solutions engineer doing a live 15-minute demo with a customer.
- Internal demoers who need a reliable smoke path for conferences or video recordings.

Success metrics (how we measure pocket effectiveness):
- Time-to-first-success: < 12 minutes for a hello-world generation + embed.
- Demo reliability: 95% success across 20 random laptops/networks.
- Onboarding completion: 60% of trial signups reach the 30-minute prototype.
- Telemetry coverage: first-10-requests captured with debug-level traces.

What this contains (artifact list):
- A 10-minute checklist (minimal commands + expected outputs).
- A 30-minute prototype recipe (sample app + prompts + simple UI).
- First-day guardrails (telemetry flags, quota checks, fallback policy guidance).
- Snippets: curl, JS, Python, and a tiny HTML demo.
- A short troubleshooting tree (3-5 most common failures).

10-minute checklist (the canonical fast loop) — recommended order:
1) Create account and grab an API key (assume self-serve).
2) Run: curl -X POST https://api.redwood.sh/v1/generate -H "Authorization: Bearer $KEY" -d '{\"prompt\":\"Say hi\"}' ; expect a short JSON with `text` field.
3) Run embed: curl -X POST https://api.redwood.sh/v1/embed -H "Authorization: Bearer $KEY" -d '{\"input\":\"redwood quickstart\"}' ; expect numeric vector.
4) Try streaming: small node script (snippet below) — expect partial tokens in under 500ms.
5) Open dashboard -> route metrics -> verify request shows up within 30s.

Notes-to-self: If streaming does not start, check network (WS blocked) and client version (>= 0.9.4).

30-minute prototype recipe (deliverables: simple search-or-summarize demo)
- Files: app/index.html, backend/server.js, prompts/summary.txt
- Backend: one endpoint that calls Redwood generate + embed to compute relevance and then returns the top paragraph.
- Frontend: small textarea + run button showing generated result and confidence-ish score.
- Acceptance: result shown, embed length > 64 dims, latency median < 420ms in demo env.

Implementation pointers (JS/Python snippets):
- JS quick: fetch POST /v1/generate with JSON {model: 'redwood-mini', prompt: '...'}; use EventSource for streaming fallback.
- Py quick: requests.post(..., json=payload, timeout=10) ; parse `choices[0].text`.
- HTML tiny: just a fetch wrapper and console.log the JSON; keeps the demo text-heavy (no flashy UI).

Telemetry & observability (what to turn on during demo/prototype):
- client.debug=true for the first 10 requests (so we get token-level timings).
- enable local verbose logs: export RW_LOG=debug for node / RW_LOGGING=trace for python.
- Dashboard: show per-route latency and token usage; add a temporary annotation for the demo start time.

First-day guardrails (ops checklist for early adopters):
- Rate limits: shadow throttling at 2x expected demo traffic.
- Fallback model: point to a lower-cost model variant in config for overload cases.
- Quota alerts: set a billing threshold alert at 25% of free tier to avoid surprises.
- Audit logs: enable request_id passthrough from client -> backend -> Redwood for traceability.

Common failure modes (triage tree) — top 4: 
- 401 / invalid key: common with copy/paste; recommend ephemeral key regen and short video showing where to find it.
- Network stream blocked: firewall / corp proxy blocks WS; fallback to polling mode with chunked polls.
- High latency on first token: cold kernels / model warmup — suggest a warmup request after provisioning.
- Embedding dimension mismatch: user code expecting 512 but model returns 768; include explicit assert in sample app.

UX micro-rituals (things to say/do during demo to reduce friction):
- "We’ll verify in the dashboard in under a minute" (calms 'it didn't work locally' concerns).
- Use short explicit prompts in the demo; avoid open-ended tasks.
- If network acts up, switch instantly to the recorded fallback (gif/video) — keep a 30s video ready.

Developer-facing copy snippets (for README and CTA):
- README one-liner: "Clone, set RW_KEY, npm start — see a working LLM-powered app in 10 minutes."
- Troubleshooting FAQ intro: "If you run into X, check Y in 60 seconds."

Security & compliance quick notes:
- Don’t paste real PII into demo prompts. Use redacted examples.
- For customer pilots, recommend Redwood Dedicated or Private; include link to the private-deployments FAQ (CONFLUENCE-SEC-42).

Open questions / TODOs (triage this list in next sync):
- Should the pocket pipeline include an optional Docker image for strictly offline demos? (pro: reproducibility, con: extra maintenance).
- How much of the telemetry UI should we pre-populate for a demo? (graphs vs one-line key stats).
- Add an alternate 1-click cloud sandbox that auto-provisions a demo route for 24 hours. Feasibility PR required.

Linked quick assets to create (short checklist):
- 2 small scripts (node & python) for 10-minute checklist.
- Sample dataset (30 short docs) for the 30-minute prototype.
- GIF fallback recorder (30s) + link on the README.
- Minimal Dockerfile for offline demo (WIP).

Notes for handoff to content/design:
- Keep screenshots minimal and annotate where latency/usage appears in the console.
- Microcopy: replace 'generate' with 'compose' for non-tech demos (A/B test this in next sprint).

Slack anchors / references used while building this doc:
- #devexp-onramp (thread: demo-reliability-2025-02)

source.title: Three-Week Focus Sprint Orchestrator — Q2 prep (draft)
source.content:
Purpose

This doc is a working orchestrator for a concentrated, three-week company-wide alignment sprint to finalize OKRs, prep an abbreviated all-staff update, and land escalation/resourcing decisions for Q2. It's intentionally light-weight — think scrappy facilitation + a few mandatory prep artifacts — so we can close outstanding choices and ship a tight comms plan.

Summary / What success looks like
- Finalized company-level OKRs with owners and measurable KRs (published).
- Leadership has a 10-minute executive update deck and 3 prepared talking points each.
- All-staff session (60 min) runs with an interactive prioritization exercise; follow-up assignments assigned within 72 hrs.
- Critical resourcing decisions (Dedicated capacity commits, CS pilot approvals, hiring priority for Infra SRE) made and committed in JIRA.

High-level schedule (three week sprint)
Week 0 (pre-sprint, 3 business days before kickoff):
- Prep: owners fill the OKR one-pager template (1 side) and attach quantitative baselines.
- Slack thread: #clarity-week — list open decisions (tag: decision-required).
- Leadership pre-brief (30m): reviewers highlight 2 red-lines per objective.

Week 1 (alignment & draft closure):
- Day 1: Cross-functional review table (60m) — owners present OKRs, constraints.
- Day 2: Deep-dive pre-reads circulated; two asynchronous votes (impact, confidence).
- Day 3: Decision session (90m) — lock wording, owners, and measurement approach.

Week 2 (resourcing & rollouts):
- Day 1: Resource alignment (finance/people/infra) — commit headcount/credits.
- Day 2: Product/Infra finalize launch/runbooks and fallback plans.
- Day 3: Draft exec update deck and run of show for All-Staff.

Week 3 (all-staff + handoff):
- Day 1: Final leadership rehearsal (45m).
- Day 2: All-staff ceremony (60m) + breakouts (30m) for team-level action mapping.
- Day 3: Publish OKRs, exec notes, and PM action items to Confluence + JIRA tickets created.

Draft company-level OKRs (starting point; each objective needs 1–2 owners and 3–4 measurable KRs)
Objective A — Make inference predictable and cheaper for production customers
- KR1: Reduce median hosted API token cost by 18% quarter-over-quarter (measurement: $/1k tokens). (owner: Ravi P.)
- KR2: Achieve 99.95% availability on Redwood Dedicated for >95% of customer traffic slots. (owner: SRE lead)
- KR3: Deliver 2 model quantization profiles in Optimize recommendations with validated regressions <2% on our eval suite. (owner: Applied ML)

Objective B — Improve developer onboarding and time-to-first-success
- KR1: Cut median onboarding time to first successful call from sign-up to working example from 72h -> 24h. (owner: DevX)
- KR2: Publish 6 new quickstart templates (including private-deploy) and measure adoption rate >= 30% within 45 days. (owner: Developer Experience)
- KR3: Lower developer-reported friction score in NPS mini-survey from 3.6 -> 4.2. (owner: Product)

Objective C — Enterprise readiness and compliance acceleration
- KR1: Complete SOC2 gap list with remediation owners and close 80% of items. (owner: Security & Compliance)
- KR2: Ship private-deploy turnkey installer v0.2 and validate in 2 customer pilots. (owner: Private Deployments)
- KR3: Publish formal SLA matrix with latency targets and pricing for Dedicated. (owner: Product & Finance)

Notes on metrics: baseline numbers are TBD — engineering leads/sdrs to add current values when they fill the one-pager. If baseline missing, tag as BLOCKER in #clarity-week.

All-Staff run-of-show (60 min) — condensed choreography
- 0:00–0:05 Welcome + context (CEO) — 3 lines: why this quarter matters, 1 quick win callout, the expectation of decisions.
- 0:05–0:15 Executive highlights (x3 leaders, 3 min each) — focus: risks, where we need cross-team help, and a single ask.
- 0:15–0:30 Interactive prioritization poll (Menti/Slido) — company-level tradeoffs: latency vs cost vs reliability. Quick breakout into 6 rooms.
- 0:30–0:45 Breakout reports (30s per room) + one-sentence commitments.
- 0:45–0:55 Key operational decisions (Ops/Infra) — what we are committing to buy/stop/hire.
- 0:55–1:00 Close + next steps (VP Ops) — where to find the published OKRs, action owners, and timeline.

Prep checklist (must be actionable and small)
- Owners: fill OKR one-pager by Day -1. Template in linked_artifacts.
- SRE/Infra: provide rough capacity delta for Dedicated requests by Week 1 Day 4.
- Finance: provide commit-level cost delta for proposed Dedicated capacity by Week 2 Day 2.
- DevX: ensure quickstart templates listed and a link is ready in the all-staff pre-read.
- Comms: prepare 1-page pre-read and a short internal blog post draft.

Decisions that need to happen in sprint (explicit)
- Approve two Dedicated capacity tiers for pilot customers (Finance + Infra).
- Greenlight private-deploy installer pilot for 2 named accounts (Sales + Legal sign-off).
- Prioritize infra hiring for SRE vs. Applied ML infra contractor (headcount team decision).

Risk register (top 6)
- Risk: Missing baselines -> measurement noise. Mitigation: owners required to fill baselines or escalate; otherwise tag KR as 'qualitative'.
- Risk: Leadership bandwidth — execs double-booked. Mitigation: 2 rehearsals, asynchronous sign-offs allowed for small wording changes.
- Risk: Too many OKRs (scope creep). Mitigation: cap to 6 company KRs; every extra KR needs 2-exec sign-off.
- Risk: Resource commits slip. Mitigation: Finance to post cost delta and an approval deadline (Week 2 Day 2).
- Risk: All-staff interactivity fails (poll tool outage). Mitigation: backup Google Forms + manual tally.
- Risk: Customer escalations during sprint consuming capacity. Mitigation: designate incident hot-seat rotations (SRE) and freeze non-critical ship decisions for 48 hrs.

Facilitator notes / scripts (rough)
- Opening: prompt the CEO to tell a 60-second customer story that connects to the first objective (cost predictability). Real story wins.
- Poll phrasing: "If you could spend one resource on Q2 to deliver the most impact for customers, would you pick: a) Latency, b) Cost, c) Reliability, d) Developer UX".
- Breakout prompt: "Name one thing your team will stop doing this quarter to free up capacity for the chosen KR". Capture in a shared doc.

Post-sprint handoff (72 hours after all-staff)
- Publish: final OKRs, executive one-pagers, and the recording.
- JIRA: PMs create ticket per KR with acceptance criteria and a 45-day short milestone. Tag: OKR-Q2-2025.
- Follow-up: PM-run retro after 21 days to check signals and drift.

Open questions / TODOs (to resolve in pre-sprint)
- Who is the final approver for infra headcount — CFO or VP Eng? (TODO: Confirm by Day -2)
- Confirm guest list for the All-Staff HQ location (hybrid logistics).
- Baseline numbers: infra and finance to fill by Day -1 or mark as qualitative.

Quick exec update talking points (for 10-min slot)
- 1: Progress snapshot: what we shipped last quarter that materially changed unit economics.
- 2: What we will commit to this quarter and why it's safe to do so (risk + mitigation).
- 3: One thing we need everyone to stop doing that frees capacity.

Appendix: templates and links (placeholders)
- OKR one-pager template: see linked_artifacts.
- All-staff pre-read checklist: 1 page (Comms to paste into Confluence).
- Recording + deck template: GitHub / decks repo link.

Rough owners for next steps
- Sprint lead & facilitator: Ava Chen (company-ops)
- OKR consolidation: Ravi Patel (Product)
- All-staff logistics & comms: Maya Gonzalez (People & Comms)
- Technical runbooks & infra capacity: Liam O'Rourke (Infra)

- Owners: fill OKR one-pager by Day -1. Template in linked_artifacts.
- SRE/Infra: provide rough capacity delta for Dedicated requests by Week 1 Day 4.
- Finance: provide commit-level cost delta for proposed Dedicated capacity by Week 2 Day 2.
- DevX: ensure quickstart templates listed and a link is ready in the all-staff pre-read.
- Comms: prepare 1-page pre-read and a short internal blog post draft.

Decisions that need to happen in sprint (explicit)
- Approve two Dedicated capacity tiers for pilot customers (Finance + Infra).
- Greenlight private-deploy installer pilot for 2 named accounts (Sales + Legal sign-off).
- Prioritize infra hiring for SRE vs. Applied ML infra contractor (headcount team decision).

Risk register (top 6)
- Risk: Missing baselines -> measurement noise. Mitigation: owners required to fill baselines or escalate; otherwise tag KR as 'qualitative'.
- Risk: Leadership bandwidth — execs double-booked. Mitigation: 2 rehearsals, asynchronous sign-offs allowed for small wording changes.
- Risk: Too many OKRs (scope creep). Mitigation: cap to 6 company KRs; every extra KR needs 2-exec sign-off.
- Risk: Resource commits slip. Mitigation: Finance to post cost delta and an approval deadline (Week 2 Day 2).
- Risk: All-staff interactivity fails (poll tool outage). Mitigation: backup Google Forms + manual tally.
- Risk: Customer escalations during sprint consuming capacity. Mitigation: designate incident hot-seat rotations (SRE) and freeze non-critical ship decisions for 48 hrs.

Facilitator notes / scripts (rough)
- Opening: prompt the CEO to tell a 60-second customer story that connects to the first objective (cost predictability). Real story wins.
- Poll phrasing: "If you could spend one resource on Q2 to deliver the most impact for customers, would you pick: a) Latency, b) Cost, c) Reliability, d) Developer UX".
- Breakout prompt: "Name one thing your team will stop doing this quarter to free up capacity for the chosen KR". Capture in a shared doc.

Post-sprint handoff (72 hours after all-staff)
- Publish: final OKRs, executive one-pagers, and the recording.
- JIRA: PMs create ticket per KR with acceptance criteria and a 45-day short milestone. Tag: OKR-Q2-2025.
- Follow-up: PM-run retro after 21 days to check signals and drift.

Open questions / TODOs (to resolve in pre-sprint)
- Who is the final approver for infra headcount — CFO or VP Eng? (TODO: Confirm by Day -2)
- Confirm guest list for the All-Staff HQ location (hybrid logistics).
- Baseline numbers: infra and finance to fill by Day -1 or mark as qualitative.

Quick exec update talking points (for 10-min slot)
- 1: Progress snapshot: what we shipped last quarter that materially changed unit economics.
- 2: What we will commit to this quarter and why it's safe to do so (risk + mitigation).
- 3: One thing we need everyone to stop doing that frees capacity.

Appendix: templates and links (placeholders)
- OKR one-pager template: see linked_artifacts.
- All-staff pre-read checklist: 1 page (Comms to paste into Confluence).
- Recording + deck template: GitHub / decks repo link.

Rough owners for next steps
- Sprint lead & facilitator: Ava Chen (company-ops)
- OKR consolidation: Ravi Patel (Product)
- All-staff logistics & comms: Maya Gonzalez (People & Comms)
- Technical runbooks & infra capacity: Liam O'Rourke (Infra)
text:
Purpose

This doc is a working orchestrator for a concentrated, three-week company-wide alignment sprint to finalize OKRs, prep an abbreviated all-staff update, and land escalation/resourcing decisions for Q2. It's intentionally light-weight — think scrappy facilitation + a few mandatory prep artifacts — so we can close outstanding choices and ship a tight comms plan.

Summary / What success looks like
- Finalized company-level OKRs with owners and measurable KRs (published).
- Leadership has a 10-minute executive update deck and 3 prepared talking points each.
- All-staff session (60 min) runs with an interactive prioritization exercise; follow-up assignments assigned within 72 hrs.
- Critical resourcing decisions (Dedicated capacity commits, CS pilot approvals, hiring priority for Infra SRE) made and committed in JIRA.

High-level schedule (three week sprint)
Week 0 (pre-sprint, 3 business days before kickoff):
- Prep: owners fill the OKR one-pager template (1 side) and attach quantitative baselines.
- Slack thread: #clarity-week — list open decisions (tag: decision-required).
- Leadership pre-brief (30m): reviewers highlight 2 red-lines per objective.

Week 1 (alignment & draft closure):
- Day 1: Cross-functional review table (60m) — owners present OKRs, constraints.
- Day 2: Deep-dive pre-reads circulated; two asynchronous votes (impact, confidence).
- Day 3: Decision session (90m) — lock wording, owners, and measurement approach.

Week 2 (resourcing & rollouts):
- Day 1: Resource alignment (finance/people/infra) — commit headcount/credits.
- Day 2: Product/Infra finalize launch/runbooks and fallback plans.
- Day 3: Draft exec update deck and run of show for All-Staff.

Week 3 (all-staff + handoff):
- Day 1: Final leadership rehearsal (45m).
- Day 2: All-staff ceremony (60m) + breakouts (30m) for team-level action mapping.
- Day 3: Publish OKRs, exec notes, and PM action items to Confluence + JIRA tickets created.

Draft company-level OKRs (starting point; each objective needs 1–2 owners and 3–4 measurable KRs)
Objective A — Make inference predictable and cheaper for production customers
- KR1: Reduce median hosted API token cost by 18% quarter-over-quarter (measurement: $/1k tokens). (owner: Ravi P.)
- KR2: Achieve 99.95% availability on Redwood Dedicated for >95% of customer traffic slots. (owner: SRE lead)
- KR3: Deliver 2 model quantization profiles in Optimize recommendations with validated regressions <2% on our eval suite. (owner: Applied ML)

Objective B — Improve developer onboarding and time-to-first-success
- KR1: Cut median onboarding time to first successful call from sign-up to working example from 72h -> 24h. (owner: DevX)
- KR2: Publish 6 new quickstart templates (including private-deploy) and measure adoption rate >= 30% within 45 days. (owner: Developer Experience)
- KR3: Lower developer-reported friction score in NPS mini-survey from 3.6 -> 4.2. (owner: Product)

Objective C — Enterprise readiness and compliance acceleration
- KR1: Complete SOC2 gap list with remediation owners and close 80% of items. (owner: Security & Compliance)
- KR2: Ship private-deploy turnkey installer v0.2 and validate in 2 customer pilots. (owner: Private Deployments)
- KR3: Publish formal SLA matrix with latency targets and pricing for Dedicated. (owner: Product & Finance)

Notes on metrics: baseline numbers are TBD — engineering leads/sdrs to add current values when they fill the one-pager. If baseline missing, tag as BLOCKER in #clarity-week.

All-Staff run-of-show (60 min) — condensed choreography
- 0:00–0:05 Welcome + context (CEO) — 3 lines: why this quarter matters, 1 quick win callout, the expectation of decisions.
- 0:05–0:15 Executive highlights (x3 leaders, 3 min each) — focus: risks, where we need cross-team help, and a single ask.
- 0:15–0:30 Interactive prioritization poll (Menti/Slido) — company-level tradeoffs: latency vs cost vs reliability. Quick breakout into 6 rooms.
- 0:30–0:45 Breakout reports (30s per room) + one-sentence commitments.
- 0:45–0:55 Key operational decisions (Ops/Infra) — what we are committing to buy/stop/hire.
- 0:55–1:00 Close + next steps (VP Ops) — where to find the published OKRs, action owners, and timeline.

Prep checklist (must be actionable and small)
- Owners: fill OKR one-pager by Day -1. Template in linked_artifacts.
- SRE/Infra: provide rough capacity delta for Dedicated requests by Week 1 Day 4.
- Finance: provide commit-level cost delta for proposed Dedicated capacity by Week 2 Day 2.
- DevX: ensure quickstart templates listed and a link is ready in the all-staff pre-read.
- Comms: prepare 1-page pre-read and a short internal blog post draft.

Decisions that need to happen in sprint (explicit)
- Approve two Dedicated capacity tiers for pilot customers (Finance + Infra).
- Greenlight private-deploy installer pilot for 2 named accounts (Sales + Legal sign-off).
- Prioritize infra hiring for SRE vs. Applied ML infra contractor (headcount team decision).

Risk register (top 6)
- Risk: Missing baselines -> measurement noise. Mitigation: owners required to fill baselines or escalate; otherwise tag KR as 'qualitative'.
- Risk: Leadership bandwidth — execs double-booked. Mitigation: 2 rehearsals, asynchronous sign-offs allowed for small wording changes.
- Risk: Too many OKRs (scope creep). Mitigation: cap to 6 company KRs; every extra KR needs 2-exec sign-off.
- Risk: Resource commits slip. Mitigation: Finance to post cost delta and an approval deadline (Week 2 Day 2).
- Risk: All-staff interactivity fails (poll tool outage). Mitigation: backup Google Forms + manual tally.
- Risk: Customer escalations during sprint consuming capacity. Mitigation: designate incident hot-seat rotations (SRE) and freeze non-critical ship decisions for 48 hrs.

Facilitator notes / scripts (rough)
- Opening: prompt the CEO to tell a 60-second customer story that connects to the first objective (cost predictability). Real story wins.
- Poll phrasing: "If you could spend one resource on Q2 to deliver the most impact for customers, would you pick: a) Latency, b) Cost, c) Reliability, d) Developer UX".
- Breakout prompt: "Name one thing your team will stop doing this quarter to free up capacity for the chosen KR". Capture in a shared doc.

Post-sprint handoff (72 hours after all-staff)

- JIRA: PMs create ticket per KR with acceptance criteria and a 45-day short milestone. Tag: OKR-Q2-2025.
- Follow-up: PM-run retro after 21 days to check signals and drift.

Open questions / TODOs (to resolve in pre-sprint)
- Who is the final approver for infra headcount — CFO or VP Eng? (TODO: Confirm by Day -2)
- Confirm guest list for the All-Staff HQ location (hybrid logistics).
- Baseline numbers: infra and finance to fill by Day -1 or mark as qualitative.

Quick exec update talking points (for 10-min slot)

- 2: What we will commit to this quarter and why it's safe to do so (risk + mitigation).
- 3: One thing we need everyone to stop doing that frees capacity.

source.title: Backstage All-Hands: Roleplay Cues, Incident Playbook & Micro-OKR Sync
source.body:
Purpose:
This is the backstage runbook for the next company All-Hands (Townline). It merges three things into a single working doc: (1) the rehearsal script and speaker cues for the executive slate, (2) an incident playbook specific to interruptions during the live meeting, and (3) a compact micro-OKR alignment checklist for post-all-hands follow-up. Primary aim is to reduce last-minute ambiguity and ensure all contingencies are owned.

Audience:
- Execs and presenters (stage-facing)
- MC / emcee and producer (AV & comms)
- Company-ops & comms leads responsible for post-handoff actions
- SRE on-call and infra contacts for live demos

Context and constraints:
- Format: 60 minutes total (45 min presentation + 15 min live Q&A).
- Hybrid event: in-office studio + live stream to global offices; pre-recorded fallback available for demos.
- Primary focus: company priorities for the quarter (micro-OKRs), progress highlights, and two short product demos.
- Acceptable risk posture: tolerate minor delays (<= 3 min), avoid demo-only stalls longer than 5 min—switch to recorded clip.

Run of show (compact):
- 0:00 – 0:03 — Emcee welcome, housekeeping, SLOs for meeting (timeboxes, how to ask questions, recording notice)
- 0:03 – 0:10 — CEO 5-7m opening + 2m “Call to focus” (top 3 company priorities)
- 0:10 – 0:22 — Product direction + 1 demo (live) — product lead presents (10m)
- 0:22 – 0:30 — Engineering highlights: latency & cost wins (8m)
- 0:30 – 0:36 — Customer stories / CS spotlight (6m)
- 0:36 – 0:44 — Ops & reliability: new Dedicated commitments and SLA changes (8m)
- 0:44 – 0:52 — Research & Optimize brief (8m)
- 0:52 – 1:00 — Live Q&A moderated by emcee (8m) + closing

Pre-show checklist (producer):
- Confirmation 48h prior: all speakers have slide decks + 2 rehearsed talking points each.
- A/V test 30m prior: video, mic, captioning, room lighting.
- Slack channel #allhands-live created and monitored by comms.
- Recorded demo clips uploaded and verified (3 quality checks) and reachable at the linked_artifacts URLs.

Speaker cues (brief and actionable):
- CEO (opening): "Lead with one customer story, then pivot to the top three company priorities — why they matter this quarter." Keep to 5–7 minutes. If time slips, cut the final anecdote.
- Product lead (demo 1): "Start with the one-sentence value prop, 60s live flow, then the 90s critical measurement (latency/cost). If demo fails, stop and cue recorded clip C1 immediately."
- Engineering presenter (latency/cost): "Show the before/after chart (p95 latency, token cost). Keep technical detail to 2 slides. If the live metrics dashboard times out, share static screenshot S1 and call out planned follow-ups."

Roleplay snippets for rehearsal (use during the 3x run-throughs):
- Emcee: "We have 10 minutes until stage — sound check ready? Producer: confirm. SRE: can you hold for a potential demo rollback?"
- Presenter: "If I lose audio mid-demo, do not attempt to keep talking — pause and hand to emcee to transition to recorded."

Incident playbook (interactive, stage-specific):
- Failure class A — presenter device disconnect / remote feed lost:
  - Immediate action: Emcee pauses, announces a 60s break while producer confirms fallback.
  - Escalation: Producer pings infra lead (SRE on-call) + comms.
  - Recovery options (choose one): (a) swap to recorded demo C1; (b) switch to next speaker; (c) quick Q&A served by emcee.
  - Messaging line: "We're switching to a short pre-recorded clip while we resolve an AV hiccup — thanks for bearing with us."

- Failure class B — live metrics dashboard times out or shows incorrect data:
  - Immediate action: Engineering presenter displays static screenshot S1 and summarizes the expected state.
  - Post-meeting action: Create a postmortem ticket (INFRA-1429) and assign to infra lead.

- Failure class C — live-stream outage (global viewers can't see the feed):
  - Immediate action: Confirm whether office audience still running. If so, continue and note to comms for follow-up. If not, pause and switch to audio-only broadcast.
  - Escalation: Communications lead drafts a short post to #allhands-live and external comms channel.

Ownership matrix during incident:
- Producer: executes fallback clip, coordinates with AV vendor, updates emcee.
- Emcee: owns live messaging tone and timing.
- SRE on-call: triage feed or infra issues; if unrelated to meeting infrastructure, declare and hand to engineering on-call.
- Comms: prepares post-event message and internal notes summarizing what viewers missed.

Micro-OKR sync (post-all-hands cadence):
- Within 24h: company-ops publishes the top 3 micro-OKRs called out in the meeting to the company Confluence page (link in linked_artifacts).
- Within 72h: each executive owner creates a 2-line plan for the quarter (owner, key metric, one immediate deliverable) and links a ticket (JIRA key).
- Within 2 weeks: product & eng create combined rollout schedule for the two showcased demos (dates, owners, rollback plan).

Follow-up template (to be sent in the all-hands Slack thread):
- TL;DR of top 3 priorities, links to slides and recorded demos, list of owners & micro-OKR bullets, where to comment or push follow-ups (Confluence / Jira).

Risk matrix (brief):
- Demo failure: Likelihood medium; impact high. Mitigation: pre-record + rehearsals + producer ownership.
- Cross-team misalignment on micro-OKR definitions: Likelihood medium; impact medium. Mitigation: 72h exec 2-line plan + follow-up alignment meeting.
- Live-stream outage: Likelihood low; impact high. Mitigation: audio fallback + comms playbook.

Decision log (live during rehearsal):
- D1: If demo fails within first 30s: always swap to recorded clip. (Owner: Producer)
- D2: If cumulative run-of-show slips by >5 minutes: cut internal segment (Research brief) and move to async deep-dive. (Owner: CEO)

Post-handoff checklist (company-ops):
- Publish meeting minutes and time-stamped video within 24h.
- Open micro-OKR tracking board and tag exec owners.
- Create follow-up tickets for any infra incidents with links to the meeting timecode.

Appendix: rehearsal schedule (recommended)
- T-3 days: dry run with all speakers, 60m. Record run so presenters can self-review.
- T-1 day: tech rehearsal (A/V only), 30m. Check captions and stream ingest endpoints.
- T-2 hours: final check with emcee, producer, and AV.

Appendix: canned messaging snippets for comms
- If we used a recorded clip: "We switched to a pre-recorded demo to keep the meeting on schedule. The team will publish a follow-up note with details and an annotated video clip for those who want the full live context."
- If stream outage: "Our primary stream encountered an intermittent outage — we will post a recording and a recap within 24 hours. Thanks for your patience."

Open questions to resolve this week (owner in parentheses):
- Do we lock the agenda at T-48h or allow one additional slide from each exec? (Ava)
- Which demo specifically has the highest risk and therefore must be pre-recorded as a primary replacement (not fallback)? (Product lead + SRE)
- Confirm captions vendor SLA and whether we need a backup (Comms)

Notes / freeform (for iteration):
- Consider a 2-minute pre-recorded "state of the world" montage to reduce dependence on a single live opening.
- If we plan to highlight Dedicated capacity pricing changes, prepare a one-slide FAQ to drop into the Slack thread to reduce immediate support churn.

Tracking links + immediate actions (today):
- COMMS-241: create #allhands-live channel and pin the follow-up template. (Owner: Priya)
- INFRA-1429: pre-create postmortem ticket for potential demo infra failures. (Owner: Ethan)
- GH PR 58: ensure recorded demo asset is merged into the internal-demos repo. (Owner: Marta)

This is a living document — please make edits inline, add additional contingencies you expect, and flag any gaps in the ownership matrix. The goal is a single source of truth so rehearsals can be minimal and the live meeting resilient.

Speaker cues (brief and actionable):
- CEO (opening): "Lead with one customer story, then pivot to the top three company priorities — why they matter this quarter." Keep to 5–7 minutes. If time slips, cut the final anecdote.
- Product lead (demo 1): "Start with the one-sentence value prop, 60s live flow, then the 90s critical measurement (latency/cost). If demo fails, stop and cue recorded clip C1 immediately."
- Engineering presenter (latency/cost): "Show the before/after chart (p95 latency, token cost). Keep technical detail to 2 slides. If the live metrics dashboard times out, share static screenshot S1 and call out planned follow-ups."

Roleplay snippets for rehearsal (use during the 3x run-throughs):
- Emcee: "We have 10 minutes until stage — sound check ready? Producer: confirm. SRE: can you hold for a potential demo rollback?"
- Presenter: "If I lose audio mid-demo, do not attempt to keep talking — pause and hand to emcee to transition to recorded."

Incident playbook (interactive, stage-specific):
- Failure class A — presenter device disconnect / remote feed lost:
  - Immediate action: Emcee pauses, announces a 60s break while producer confirms fallback.
  - Escalation: Producer pings infra lead (SRE on-call) + comms.
  - Recovery options (choose one): (a) swap to recorded demo C1; (b) switch to next speaker; (c) quick Q&A served by emcee.
  - Messaging line: "We're switching to a short pre-recorded clip while we resolve an AV hiccup — thanks for bearing with us."

- Failure class B — live metrics dashboard times out or shows incorrect data:
  - Immediate action: Engineering presenter displays static screenshot S1 and summarizes the expected state.
  - Post-meeting action: Create a postmortem ticket (INFRA-1429) and assign to infra lead.

- Failure class C — live-stream outage (global viewers can't see the feed):
  - Immediate action: Confirm whether office audience still running. If so, continue and note to comms for follow-up. If not, pause and switch to audio-only broadcast.
  - Escalation: Communications lead drafts a short post to #allhands-live and external comms channel.

Ownership matrix during incident:
- Producer: executes fallback clip, coordinates with AV vendor, updates emcee.
- Emcee: owns live messaging tone and timing.
- SRE on-call: triage feed or infra issues; if unrelated to meeting infrastructure, declare and hand to engineering on-call.
- Comms: prepares post-event message and internal notes summarizing what viewers missed.

Micro-OKR sync (post-all-hands cadence):
- Within 24h: company-ops publishes the top 3 micro-OKRs called out in the meeting to the company Confluence page (link in linked_artifacts).
- Within 72h: each executive owner creates a 2-line plan for the quarter (owner, key metric, one immediate deliverable) and links a ticket (JIRA key).
- Within 2 weeks: product & eng create combined rollout schedule for the two showcased demos (dates, owners, rollback plan).

Follow-up template (to be sent in the all-hands Slack thread):
- TL;DR of top 3 priorities, links to slides and recorded demos, list of owners & micro-OKR bullets, where to comment or push follow-ups (Confluence / Jira).

Risk matrix (brief):
- Demo failure: Likelihood medium; impact high. Mitigation: pre-record + rehearsals + producer ownership.
- Cross-team misalignment on micro-OKR definitions: Likelihood medium; impact medium. Mitigation: 72h exec 2-line plan + follow-up alignment meeting.
- Live-stream outage: Likelihood low; impact high. Mitigation: audio fallback + comms playbook.

Decision log (live during rehearsal):
- D1: If demo fails within first 30s: always swap to recorded clip. (Owner: Producer)
- D2: If cumulative run-of-show slips by >5 minutes: cut internal segment (Research brief) and move to async deep-dive. (Owner: CEO)

Post-handoff checklist (company-ops):
- Publish meeting minutes and time-stamped video within 24h.
- Open micro-OKR tracking board and tag exec owners.
- Create follow-up tickets for any infra incidents with links to the meeting timecode.

Appendix: rehearsal schedule (recommended)
- T-3 days: dry run with all speakers, 60m. Record run so presenters can self-review.
- T-1 day: tech rehearsal (A/V only), 30m. Check captions and stream ingest endpoints.
- T-2 hours: final check with emcee, producer, and AV.

Appendix: canned messaging snippets for comms
- If we used a recorded clip: "We switched to a pre-recorded demo to keep the meeting on schedule. The team will publish a follow-up note with details and an annotated video clip for those who want the full live context."
- If stream outage: "Our primary stream encountered an intermittent outage — we will post a recording and a recap within 24 hours. Thanks for your patience."

Open questions to resolve this week (owner in parentheses):
- Do we lock the agenda at T-48h or allow one additional slide from each exec? (Ava)
- Which demo specifically has the highest risk and therefore must be pre-recorded as a primary replacement (not fallback)? (Product lead + SRE)
- Confirm captions vendor SLA and whether we need a backup (Comms)

Notes / freeform (for iteration):
- Consider a 2-minute pre-recorded "state of the world" montage to reduce dependence on a single live opening.
- If we plan to highlight Dedicated capacity pricing changes, prepare a one-slide FAQ to drop into the Slack thread to reduce immediate support churn.

Tracking links + immediate actions (today):
- COMMS-241: create #allhands-live channel and pin the follow-up template. (Owner: Priya)
- INFRA-1429: pre-create postmortem ticket for potential demo infra failures. (Owner: Ethan)
- GH PR 58: ensure recorded demo asset is merged into the internal-demos repo. (Owner: Marta)

This is a living document — please make edits inline, add additional contingencies you expect, and flag any gaps in the ownership matrix. The goal is a single source of truth so rehearsals can be minimal and the live meeting resilient.

source.title: 50-day Huddle Cadence & All-Hands Prep Manifest
source.body:
Purpose
This doc collects the operational plan and simple playbook for the next 50 days leading into the Q2 all-hands and exec update. It is intentionally lightweight: a cadence, owners, key messages to test, and a short OKR draft that we can iterate in weekly huddles.

Audience
- Execs (read-ahead)
- People leads + ICs running demos
- Comms & GTM for alignment

Summary timeline (50-day window)
- Day 0 (2025-02-10): Draft circulated (this doc)
- Week 1: Leadership read-ahead + feedback (2025-02-11 to 2025-02-17)
- Weeks 2-4: Team huddles (weekly), consolidate one-pagers, capture 3 demos
- Week 5: Exec dry run + comms review
- Day 50 (2025-03-31): All-hands + published exec update

Cadence (practical)
- Weekly 45-minute cross-functional huddle (Mon 09:30 PT) — agenda: progress vs action log, hot blockers, demo signups, message test results.
- Bi-weekly OKR review with exec sponsors (30m)
- Two micro-sessions (30m) for demo rehearsals and QA the week before runway

Who owns what (quick map)
- Program lead: Ava Chen — orchestration, meeting notes, deck collate
- OKR shepherd: Diego Morales — collect metric inputs, draft language
- Comms lead: Priya Kapoor — announcement cadence, townline script
- Demo coordinator: Jasmine Park — slot booking, runbook for presenters
- SRE liaison: Liam O'Neill — readiness checklist (prod demos), incident fallback plan

Draft OKRs (starter language — to be iterated in huddles)
- Objective A: Reduce inference tail-latency variability for hosted API customers
  - KR1: Decrease 95th percentile token-latency by 20% (baseline = internal perf week 2025-01)
  - KR2: Ship two routing fallback policies and enable for top-5 customers by EoQ
  - KR3: Document and roll out KV-cache configuration templates for three canonical workloads

- Objective B: Lower per-token unit cost for common PLG workloads
  - KR1: Evaluate and pilot 2 quantization profiles on staging with perf/regression guardrails
  - KR2: Deploy automatic batch-sizing suggestion in Optimize for top-tier projects
  - KR3: Achieve 8% cost reduction on synthetic workload used by growth team

- Objective C: Improve product reliability & visibility for Dedicated customers
  - KR1: Publish SLA playbook and run a canary with one pilot customer
  - KR2: Add token-level cost traces to Console for dedicated routes
  - KR3: Reduce mean time to recovery (MTTR) for infra incidents by 30% in pilot teams

Draft exec update bullets (one-liners for slides)
- Production: 95p latency down 10% in beta, routing fallback shipped as beta policy
- Cost: pilot quantization reduced synthetic cost by ~6% (early)
- Adoption: 3 Dedicated pilots onboarded, one private deploy contract in negotiation
- Risks: KB cache regressions on long-context streams; mitigation: automated eval + rollback plan

All-hands planning (asks + slots)
- 25-minute leadership narrative (CEO/CTO) — theme: "Serving as the product" (why inference ops matter)
- 3x 8-minute demo slots (serving runtime, Console new traces, Optimize tooling)
- Q&A: crowdsource top-10 questions from team form, moderated by People Ops
- Breakout rooms: product roadmap micro-sessions (followups, 20m)

Prep checklist (immediate next steps)
- [ ] Diego: consolidate metric sources by 2025-02-14 (Jira PLAT-4821)
- [ ] Priya: draft announcement + townline script v0.1 by 2025-02-18
- [ ] Jasmine: confirm demo slots and required infra by 2025-02-21
- [ ] Liam: produce runbook + rollback plan for each demo by 2025-02-24
- [ ] All: add 1 slide each team for the leadership deck by 2025-03-07

Open questions / risks (for weekly huddle)
- Demo stability: which demos absolutely require private-deploy staging vs hosted staging?
- Customer sensitivity: what product info can we share publicly during all-hands about pilots?
- Data: are we confident in metric baselines for latency/cost? (Diego to validate)

Notes to self (informal)
- Keep slides short, favor 1 metric per slide. Execs like crisp signals + change requests.
- Consider adding micro-postmortem slot in huddle if a demo fails — reduces stress and stigma.

Reference links (quick)
- Draft OKR doc: confluence.redwood.company/okrs/q2-2025-draft
- All-hands logistics sheet: drive/sheets/ah-logistics-2025
- Runbook template: github.com/redwood-inference/runbooks/template
source.notes: Working doc: expect many iterations. If you want to change OKR language, bring proposed metric and owner to Monday huddle.

source.title: Polyglot client kickstart playflow
source.summary: Hands-on quickstart and onboarding playflow for a minimal, language-agnostic Redwood client. Focus: 15-minute developer path (Python/JS/Go), telemetry contract, minimal sample app, and rollout checklist for enabling teams to call the hosted API with standard retries, streaming, and auth patterns.
goals: Deliver a reproducible 15-minute path to a first successful call from any supported language
Define a single minimal telemetry contract that works across languages and runtime constraints
Ship a tiny sample app and smoke tests that CI can run
Identify anti-patterns and common failure modes to document in onboarding
source.audience: Software engineers and platform engineers who are integrating Redwood inference into product flows; SDK maintainers who will extend client shims for new languages; Solutions engineers running demos.
prereqs: Redwood API key with developer sandbox access
Node 18+ for JS sample, Python 3.11+ for Python sample, Go 1.20+ for Go sample (optional)
Optional: local tunnel (ngrok or cloudflared) for webhook/streaming demo
quickstart_steps: Step 0 — clone repo: git clone git@github.com:redwood-inference/playground-starters.git && cd playground-starters/polyglot-kickstart
Step 1 — set env: cp .env.example .env && export REDWOOD_API_KEY=sk_dev_XXX (Mac/Linux) or setx REDWOOD_API_KEY "sk_dev_XXX" (Windows)
Step 2 — run language sample (JS): cd js && npm ci && npm run demo (this performs a single streaming chat request)
Step 3 — run language sample (Python): cd ../python && python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate && pip install -r requirements.txt && python demo.py
Step 4 — run language sample (Go): cd ../go && go run ./cmd/demo/main.go (requires GOPATH or modules enabled)
Step 5 — smoke test: ./scripts/smoke.sh (runs minimal curl requests and validates response shapes and telemetry events emitted to the local collector)
example_commands: # JS streaming demo (uses fetch and EventSource shim): npm run demo -- --prompt "Summarize the last meeting in 3 bullets"
# Python non-streaming: python demo.py --prompt "Give a short product blurb for Redwood Inference"
# Curl quick-check: curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY" -d '{"model":"rwd-small-1","input":"hello"}' https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate | jq -r .output
sample_app_structure: /js: tiny express app with a single /chat endpoint and EventSource-based streaming client
/python: flask-based demo that shows sync and async call patterns and a unit test for output shape
/go: minimal cli that demonstrates header auth and reconnect logic
/tests: integration smoke tests that run against the sandbox API key in CI with mocked rate limits
telemetry_contract: Event: rwd.client.call — emitted synchronously for every request

Fields: event_id (uuid v4), timestamp (iso8601), sdk_lang (js|py|go), sdk_version (semver), model (string), call_type (sync|stream), latency_ms (int), status (ok|error|partial), token_count (int, optional), error_code (string, optional)
Event: rwd.client.retry — emitted for any automatic retry; fields: parent_event_id, attempt (int), reason (rate_limit|transient|network)
Event: rwd.client.fallback — when routing falls back to a cheaper/compatible model; fields: original_model, fallback_model, reason
Transport: events should be buffered and sent in batches; SDKs must support a local file-backed buffer when network unavailable
Privacy: PII must never be sent in telemetry events; provide explicit opt-out via REDWOOD_TELEMETRY=false
rollout_plan: Phase 0 (internal): ship playbook and samples to eng-devx team and collect feedback (1 sprint)
Phase 1 (beta): enable samples in the public repo with a "Try it in REPL" GH Action that runs smoke tests on PRs (2 sprints)
Phase 2 (default): mark SDK shims as supported in the API docs, add telemetry ingestion dashboard and alerting, announce via newsletters and office hours (3 sprints)
metrics_and_sla: First-call time to success (target): < 15 minutes for a brand new engineer following the playflow
Smoke test pass rate in CI (target): 98% across platforms
Telemetry event delivery (target): 99% of events from internal CI runs should appear in the devx telemetry dashboard
SDK error rate (after GA): < 0.5% of API calls in sample apps (excluding intentional chaos tests)
anti_patterns: Embedding full user PII into telemetry events to circumvent contract — must not happen
Relying on a heavy language-specific dependency tree in samples (avoid large frameworks) — keep samples minimal
Spawning unbounded retry loops without exponential backoff — use exponential jittered backoff and a max attempts limit
Treating streaming as a drop-in replacement for sync without documenting partial-chunk semantics and reconnection behavior
open_questions: Should we provide a built-in local telemetry collector in each SDK or a single cross-language tiny binary? Pros/cons: developer friction vs maintenance surface.
What is the minimum supported runtime for mobile (iOS/Android) lightweight clients — do we need a separate mobile playflow?
Do we include an opinionated HTTP client wrapper (retry/timeout) in every SDK or just provide patterns and reference implementation?
How will billing-sensitive telemetry (token_count) be handled for customers who disable telemetry? Provide an explicit opt-in for billing telemetry?
source.notes: Next actions: assign an owner for the telemetry collector prototype (Ethan), draft API docs snippets for the telemetry contract (Miguel), and schedule a 30-minute sync with Solutions Engineering to validate the 15-minute path with a non-engineer demo (Lila). Keep this doc intentionally lightweight; migrate to Confluence once stable.

source.title: Sprint 42 — inference fanout & dependency boundaries meeting notes
source.content:
Meeting: Sprint 42 deep-dive — inference fanout, dependency boundaries, and graceful degradation
Date: 2025-02-12
Attendees: Ava Chen (facilitator), Diego Morales, Priya Nair, Ethan Brooks, Rosa Kim, Alex Ford (observer)
Duration: 60m

Context / Why this meeting
- Multiple incidents last quarter where high-cardinality fanout (per-request calls to downstream scorers, rerankers, and external microservices) increased tail latency and caused cascading queueing on serving nodes.
- We need a clear architecture for: per-request fanout limits, separation of synchronous vs async dependencies, and a consistent fallback policy that preserves correctness for core use-cases.
- Tied to INF-1128 (Jira): implement routing & degradation primitives in the runtime for tenant-level isolation.

Agenda
1) Quick recap of incidents and metrics observed
2) Proposals for fanout control & dependency boundaries
3) Fallthrough/fallback strategies per dependency class
4) Impact on KV cache, batching, and kernel selection
5) Action items and owners

Recap (Diego)
- Incident summary: three spikes where per-request external scorer calls increased p99 latencies from 120ms to ~800ms; downstream services exceeded quota and timed out, causing in-flight requests to queue and bump memory.
- Observed patterns: long tail correlated with request heterogeneity (some requests trigger 5–8 external calls), token sequence length distribution unchanged.

Proposals discussed
A) Per-request fanout budget enforced by runtime (preferred)
   - Assign a numeric fanout budget to each incoming request (default 4).
   - If budget exhausted, runtime will either: i) skip optional scorers, ii) switch to lightweight local heuristics, or iii) queue with strict deadline.
   - Budgets configurable per-tenant via route config and dynamic feature flags.

B) Dependency classification & boundary layer (agreed complement)
   - Classify dependencies into: core-sync (must finish before token emit), optional-sync (affects ranking but can be skipped), async-post (post-response enrichment), and external-batchable (can be pooled).
   - Introduce a boundary layer in front of downstream clients that enforces timeouts, circuits, and circuit-breaker metrics.

C) Local surrogate models for optional dependencies (exploratory)
   - Small quantized models or heuristic rules used when remote scorer unavailable to preserve result quality.
   - Tradeoff: increased local CPU/GPU cost vs reduced tail risk.

Decisions made (consensus)
- Adopt proposal A + B together: runtime-enforced fanout budgets + explicit dependency classification layer.
- Default fanout budget = 4 for hosted API routes. Dedicated/Private deployments can set higher defaults.
- Optional-sync dependencies will be skipped when budget is exhausted; async-post will always be used when possible but must not block final token emission.
- Implement a pluggable "surrogate" registry for optional dependencies to allow teams to provide local fallbacks; initially, teams can opt-in (no global rollout).

Rationale / tradeoffs discussed
- Enforcing budget in runtime is simpler than requiring every service to self-rate-limit; central enforcement reduces cross-team misconfiguration.
- Switching to local surrogates reduces external call surface but increases resource footprint on serving nodes; therefore opt-in to control costs.
- Strict deadlines + fast-path local heuristics keep p99 latency predictable for SLAs.

Design notes & implementation sketch
- Request metadata: extend request envelope with fanout_budget:int, budget_consumed:int, and dependency_flags:list.
- Runtime interceptors: a lightweight interceptor checks budget before issuing secondary calls; decrements budget atomically. If budget insufficient, raises a canonical SkipDependency error returned to higher layer.
- Dependency client wrappers: each downstream client must implement a small adapter that returns (result | SkipMarker) and annotates cost (1 by default).
- Circuit-breaker: library using EWMA latency + error-rate thresholds; on open, mark dependency as degraded for tenant & route.
- Metrics: new metrics: fanout_budget_assigned, fanout_budget_used, dependency_skips_total{dependency,route,tenant}, surrogate_fallbacks_total.

Compatibility & effects on other systems
- KV cache: when optional-sync is skipped, cached KV context remains the same; ensure we do not invalidate KV shards on fallback paths.
- Batching: secondary calls currently batched by a shard key; skipping will reduce batch fullness and may change throughput. We will track batched throughput delta.
- Kernel selection: no immediate changes; but if surrogate models are added, they may be quantized and run on CPU/GPU causing scheduler effects.

Alternatives considered and why rejected
- Letting each service self-enforce: rejected because inconsistent behavior across tenants and harder to audit.
- Global queueing with priority tiers: complex to implement and risks large queues; preferred to fail-fast/skip optional work.

title: Sprint 42 — inference fanout & dependency boundaries meeting notes
preview:
Meeting: Sprint 42 deep-dive — inference fanout, dependency boundaries, and graceful degradation
Date: 2025-02-12
Attendees: Ava Chen (facilitator), Diego Morales, Priya Nair, Ethan Brooks, Rosa Kim, Alex Ford (observer)
Duration: 60m

Context / Why this meeting
- Multiple incidents last quarter where high-cardinality fanout (per-request calls to downstream scorers, rerankers, and external microservices) increased tail latency and caused cascading queueing on serving nodes.
- We need a clear architecture for: per-request fanout limits, separation of synchronous vs async dependencies, and a consistent fallback policy that preserves correctness for core use-cases.
- Tied to INF-1128 (Jira): implement routing & degradation primitives in the runtime for tenant-level isolation.

Agenda
1) Quick recap of incidents and metrics observed
2) Proposals for fanout control & dependency boundaries
3) Fallthrough/fallback strategies per dependency class
4) Impact on KV cache, batching, and kernel selection
5) Action items and owners

Recap (Diego)
- Incident summary: three spikes where per-request external scorer calls increased p99 latencies from 120ms to ~800ms; downstream services exceeded quota and timed out, causing in-flight requests to queue and bump memory.
- Observed patterns: long tail correlated with request heterogeneity (some requests trigger 5–8 external calls), token sequence length distribution unchanged.

Proposals discussed
A) Per-request fanout budget enforced by runtime (preferred)
   - Assign a numeric fanout budget to each incoming request (default 4).
   - If budget exhausted, runtime will either: i) skip optional scorers, ii) switch to lightweight local heuristics, or iii) queue with strict deadline.
   - Budgets configurable per-tenant via route config and dynamic feature flags.

B) Dependency classification & boundary layer (agreed complement)
   - Classify dependencies into: core-sync (must finish before token emit), optional-sync (affects ranking but can be skipped), async-post (post-response enrichment), and external-batchable (can be pooled).
   - Introduce a boundary layer in front of downstream clients that enforces timeouts, circuits, and circuit-breaker metrics.

C) Local surrogate models for optional dependencies (exploratory)
   - Small quantized models or heuristic rules used when remote scorer unavailable to preserve result quality.
   - Tradeoff: increased local CPU/GPU cost vs reduced tail risk.

Decisions made (consensus)
- Adopt proposal A + B together: runtime-enforced fanout budgets + explicit dependency classification layer.
- Default fanout budget = 4 for hosted API routes. Dedicated/Private deployments can set higher defaults.
- Optional-sync dependencies will be skipped when budget is exhausted; async-post will always be used when possible but must not block final token emission.
- Implement a pluggable "surrogate" registry for optional dependencies to allow teams to provide local fallbacks; initially, teams can opt-in (no global rollout).

Rationale / tradeoffs discussed
- Enforcing budget in runtime is simpler than requiring every service to self-rate-limit; central enforcement reduces cross-team misconfiguration.
- Switching to local surrogates reduces external call surface but increases resource footprint on serving nodes; therefore opt-in to control costs.
- Strict deadlines + fast-path local heuristics keep p99 latency predictable for SLAs.

Design notes & implementation sketch
- Request metadata: extend request envelope with fanout_budget:int, budget_consumed:int, and dependency_flags:list.
- Runtime interceptors: a lightweight interceptor checks budget before issuing secondary calls; decrements budget atomically. If budget insufficient, raises a canonical SkipDependency error returned to higher layer.
- Dependency client wrappers: each downstream client must implement a small adapter that returns (result | SkipMarker) and annotates cost (1 by default).
- Circuit-breaker: library using EWMA latency + error-rate thresholds; on open, mark dependency as degraded for tenant & route.
- Metrics: new metrics: fanout_budget_assigned, fanout_budget_used, dependency_skips_total{dependency,route,tenant}, surrogate_fallbacks_total.

Compatibility & effects on other systems
- KV cache: when optional-sync is skipped, cached KV context remains the same; ensure we do not invalidate KV shards on fallback paths.
- Batching: secondary calls currently batched by a shard key; skipping will reduce batch fullness and may change throughput. We will track batched throughput delta.
- Kernel selection: no immediate changes; but if surrogate models are added, they may be quantized and run on CPU/GPU causing scheduler effects.

Alternatives considered and why rejected
- Letting each service self-enforce: rejected because inconsistent behavior across tenants and harder to audit.
- Global queueing with priority tiers: complex to implement and risks large queues; preferred to fail-fast/skip optional work.

Open questions (need follow-ups)
- Q1: How to set per-tenant defaults dynamically based on observed behavior? (Priya to prototype a heuristics-based recommender)
- Q2: Billing & cost attribution for surrogate usage in Dedicated/Private customers — finance needs input.
- Q3: Safety: when skipping optional-sync, how do we ensure no safety regressions for content filters that are optional? (security review required)

Action items (owner — due)
- A1: Spike: runtime interceptor PoC that enforces fanout budgets (Ethan) — due 2025-02-20
- A2: Draft dependency classification guidelines and taxonomy doc (Rosa) — due 2025-02-17
- A3: Add metrics instrumentation plan and Grafana dashboard wireframe (Diego) — due 2025-02-18
- A4: Confluence page for surrogate onboarding + initial contract template (Ava) — due 2025-02-19
- A5: Security sign-off checklist for optional dependency skipping (Priya + Security) — due 2025-02-21

Follow-up meeting
- Quick sync planned for 2025-02-19 (15m) to review PoC progress and finalize metric names.

Notes to self / friction points called out in discussion
- Need clear operator-visible signals when fallback path is used (not only internal logs).
- Ensure dedicated capacity customers can opt-out of surrogate fallbacks by policy.
- Avoid adding too many flags; prefer route-level config with safe defaults.

Appendix: sketch of runtime interceptor behavior (textual)
- onRequest(r): budget = r.metadata.fanout_budget || route.defaultBudget
- for each dependency in graph:
    if budget <= 0 and dependency.class == optional-sync -> mark skipped and emit metric
    else attempt call with dependency.adapter.call().withTimeout(route.depTimeout)
       on success: budget -= adapter.cost
       on timeout/error: if adapter.hasSurrogate -> call surrogate; else mark degraded and continue
- end

End of notes

Informal: overall tone from team leaned towards conservative defaults and fast-fail behavior. Strong preference to keep core-sync small and well-instrumented.

source.title: Fleet stress ladder — $/1M token ladder and regional burn scenarios
source.content:
Purpose and scope:
This working sheet explores a stress-testing view of fleet unit economics: how GPU-hour burn maps to $/1M tokens across regions and workload mixes under normal, stressed, and optimized operating regimes. Goal is to provide supply-side knobs (reserve/spot mix, cushion, quantization) and a simple conversion template engineers and finance can use to estimate incremental spend during bursts or degradation events.

Key assumptions (editable):
- Representative GPUs: A100-80GB ("A100") and H100-80GB ("H100").
- Throughput measured as tokens/sec for a medium-to-large generation workload (2k context typical).
- Utilization = fraction of wall-clock GPU time doing useful generation (excludes maintenance, idle, warm-up).
- Batching efficiency = multiplier capturing shorter sequences, packing, and kernel-level batching wins (0-1).
- Regions modeled: us-west1 (primary), us-east1 (backup), eu-west1 (EMEA), ap-southeast1 (APAC).
- Pricing buckets: reserved (committed) and spot (preemptible). Prices are illustrative and include cloud premiums/egress amortized into per-GPU-hour.

Inputs (baseline values):
- A100 tokens/sec (baseline model): 900 tokens/sec
- H100 tokens/sec (baseline model): 1400 tokens/sec
- Baseline utilization: 0.65
- Baseline batching efficiency: 0.80
- Regional GPU-hour prices (reserved / spot):
  * us-west1: A100 $3.50 / $1.25 ; H100 $8.00 / $3.00
  * us-east1: A100 $3.40 / $1.20 ; H100 $7.80 / $2.90 (slightly cheaper)
  * eu-west1: A100 $4.10 / $1.45 ; H100 $9.20 / $3.35 (10-15% premium)
  * ap-southeast1: A100 $4.50 / $1.60 ; H100 $10.00 / $3.80 (20-25% premium)

Conversion formula (single-GPU-hour -> $/1M tokens):
- tokens_per_gpu_hour = tokens_per_sec * 3600 * utilization * batching_efficiency
- $/1M tokens = gpu_hour_cost / (tokens_per_gpu_hour / 1_000_000)

Baseline calculations (reserved pricing):
- A100: tokens/hour raw = 900 * 3600 = 3,240,000 ; effective tokens = 3,240,000 * 0.65 * 0.8 = 1,684,800 tokens/hour.
  $/1M tokens = 3.50 / (1.6848) = $2.08 per 1M tokens
- H100: tokens/hour raw = 1400 * 3600 = 5,040,000 ; effective tokens = 5,040,000 * 0.65 * 0.8 = 2,620,800 tokens/hour.
  $/1M tokens = 8.00 / (2.6208) = $3.05 per 1M tokens
Notes: baseline shows A100 reserved gives lower $/1M for this particular model because A100 reserved is comparatively cheap; H100 reserved becomes attractive when throughput scales (or for larger models with lower tokens/sec delta).

Regional reserved $/1M snapshot (baseline tokens/sec and utilization above):
- us-west1: A100=$2.08 ; H100=$3.05
- us-east1: scale A100 price by 0.97 -> A100=$2.02 ; H100=$2.95
- eu-west1: scale A100 price by 1.17 -> A100=$2.43 ; H100=$3.57
- ap-southeast1: scale A100 price by 1.29 -> A100=$2.69 ; H100=$3.86
(These are rough proportional scalings of GPU-hour to $/1M tokens using same conversion formula.)

Stress scenario (preemption, tail-latency degradation, higher cold-starts):
- Effective utilization drops to 0.50, batching efficiency drops to 0.60 (smaller batches, more padding), spot fraction increases during bursts.
- A100 effective tokens/hour = 3,240,000 * 0.5 * 0.6 = 972,000 -> reserved $/1M = 3.50 / 0.972 = $3.60 ; spot $/1M (us-west spot $1.25) = 1.25 / 0.972 = $1.29
- H100 effective tokens/hour = 5,040,000 * 0.5 * 0.6 = 1,512,000 -> reserved $/1M = 8.00 / 1.512 = $5.29 ; spot $/1M = 3.00 / 1.512 = $1.98
Interpretation: in stress windows, reserved $/1M increases ~70% for A100 and ~73% for H100 vs baseline. Spot still looks cheap per token, but spot availability and preemption risk mean effective end-to-end cost is higher (due to retries, fallbacks).

Workload-mix blended model (example):
- Mix A: 70% A100-reserved, 20% A100-spot, 10% H100-reserved (steady state)
- Weighted $/1M (us-west baseline) = 0.7*$2.08 + 0.2*$0.75*? (spot A100 $1.25 -> $/1M spot = 1.25 / 1.6848 = $0.742) + 0.1*$3.05 = compute -> 0.7*2.08 + 0.2*0.742 + 0.1*3.05 = 1.456 + 0.149 + 0.305 = $1.91 per 1M tokens
- If stress hits and the mix shifts to more reserved or bursts to H100 for latency, blended $/1M can spike >$3.2.

Burst buffer sizing guidance (rule-of-thumb):
- For a 100% RPS spike for 15 minutes, reserve cushion = (peak RPS - baseline RPS) * expected tokens per request converted to GPU-hours.
- Quick estimate: 1 A100 reserved supports ~1.68M effective tokens/hr baseline -> ~28k tokens/min. For a 15-minute 100% spike, need additional GPUs = (additional tokens in 15m) / (tokens per GPU in 15m).
- For planning add 15-25% extra capacity for ephemeral tail-latency/colocation noise.

Degradation and SLO mapping (practical ops knobs):
- If $/1M jumps > 40% for more than 30min, trigger: analyze queueing/backpressure, increase batch windows by X ms, attempt quantized model fallback, or divert non-critical traffic to cached responses.
- If spot preemptions > 20% of running batch jobs, move ephemeral traffic to H100-reserved cannibalization or multi-region fallback.

Optimization levers and expected impact (order-of-magnitude):
- Improved batching (+10 percentage points batching_eff) -> ~12% lower $/1M
- Quantized model (2x tokens/sec on same GPU) -> ~50% lower $/1M if model quality acceptable
- Better packing & prefix caching -> reduces effective tokens/generation per GPU hour wasted on overlap -> 8-18% improvement
- Geo-arbitrage (move non-sensitive traffic to cheapest region) -> 10-30% depending on traffic split and egress tradeoffs

Open questions / TODOs:
- Populate this sheet with actual month-to-date spot/reserved average prices from cloud billing (JIRA-4321).
- Run small bench matrix for the actual 3 production model variants to swap tokens/sec values into the table (owner: Rina).
- Add cold-start penalty modeling for private deployments where HSM/KMS delays add wall-clock overhead.

Quick action items for next 2 weeks:
- Diego: extract last 90 days GPU-hour pricing by region and feed into column 'price_actual'.
- Samir: run 3 model throughput runs on A100/H100 and attach raw logs.
- Ava: synthesize a short playbook for when blended $/1M token exceeds target threshold (notify, failover, scale).

Appendix: worked example (us-west1 reserved A100 baseline)
- Input: tokens/sec=900 ; util=0.65 ; batching=0.80 ; price=$3.50/hr
- tokens_per_gpu_hour = 900*3600*0.65*0.8 = 1,684,800
- $/1M = 3.50 / (1.6848) = $2.08 -> documented above

Contact & review: this is a working draft. Please leave inline comments in the Confluence page linked above or drop notes in #infra-capacity.
